coredata.dc:description: Intracellular pH is a key factor in cell homeostasis, regulated within specific compartments, and changes in pH can result from or affect biochemical pathways. This study explores a yellow fluorescent protein EYFP-G65T as a core for a time-resolved pH-indicator. Among the tested designs—a circular permutant, a chimeric SypHer3s-like construct, and an unmodified protein—the unmodified EYFP-G65T performed best for live-cell imaging. Upon two-photon excitation, purified EYFP-G65T exhibited a 4.5-fold increase in mean fluorescence lifetime across pH 5.5–7 and a 7-fold change in its major component's lifetime from pH 6.5–8. Using this indicator, we measured pH values ranging from 6 to 8 in various organelles, and mapped pH shifts in mitochondria and the Golgi apparatus in response to stimuli.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Linear schemes of the coding sequences used in the study. Fluorescent proteins or their fragments are shown in yellow, OxyR-RD – a H2O2-sensitive regulatory domain of E. coli transcription factor – in green.
pH-dependence of the ratiometric fluorescence signal of purified SypHer3s and SypHer3s-G65T samples.

Graphs describing pH-dependence of the fluorescence lifetime of EYFP-G65T at different excitation wavelengths. (A) τ1—lifetime of the short-lived component of biexponential fluorescence decay, (B) amplitude-weighted mean fluorescence lifetime.

FLIM data recorded from the monensin/nigericin-penetrated HEK293T cells expressing EYFP-G65T and pre-incubated with pH-buffers (pH 4.5–8.5). (A) Histograms showing the mean lifetime distribution calculated from the FLIM scans at different pH values (color legend is in the inset). (B) The graph describing dependence of the mean lifetime on pH. Experimental values (black squares) were fitted with a 4th order polynomial regression curve (red line). (C) Color-coded FLIM scans exemplifying the lifetime values recorded in the cytoplasm of HEK293T cells (color legend is under the image gallery). Two-photon excitation with a laser λ em = 750 nm was used

FLIM data recorded from the monensin/nigericin-penetrated HEK293T cells expressing EYFP-G65T in the cytoplasm, mitochondria matrix and Golgi apparatus and pre-incubated with pH-buffers (pH 5.0–8.5). The graph describes dependence of the amplitude-weighted mean fluorescence lifetime on pH fitted with a 4th order polynomial regression curve. Two-photon excitation with a laser λ em = 750 nm was used.

FLIM data recorded from the monensin/nigericin-penetrated HEK293T cells co-expressing EYFP-G65T in the cytoplasm and mitochondrial matrix and pre-incubated with pH-buffers (pH 6.5–8.0). (A) Color-coded FLIM scans exemplifying the lifetime values recorded in the cytoplasm and mitochondrial matrix of HEK293T cells (color legend is under the image gallery). The cytoplasmic and mitochondrial signals are shown separately in the top (C) and middle (M) rows, respectively. The separate coloration of subcellular structures is achieved by manually setting the color coding range thresholds, which are applied based on the differences in τm values. In the bottom row (C + M), the entire acquired signal is displayed. (B) The graph describing dependence of the mean lifetime on pH (the lifetime data points fitted with a 4th order polynomial regression curve). (C) Histograms showing the mean lifetime distribution calculated from the FLIM scans at different pH values (color legend is in the inset). Two-photon excitation with a laser λ em = 750 nm was used.

FLIM data recorded from HEK293T cells expressing EYFP-G65T in the Golgi apparatus. (A) Histograms showing the distribution of the probe's mean fluorescence lifetime in intact Golgi apparatus (black solid line) and after the addition of 25 mM NH4Cl (red solid line), and 10 μM CCCP (green solid line). (B) Calibration plot (τm/pH dependence measured in Golgi apparatus and fitted with a 4th order polynomial regression curve) used to determine the absolute pH values in Golgi apparatus. The mean lifetime and corresponding pH values are shown in a parenthesis near the curve. (C) Color-coded FLIM scans exemplifying the lifetime values recorded in the Golgi apparatus of HEK293T cells before and after the addition of 25 mM NH4Cl, and 10 μM CCCP (color legend is under the image gallery). Two-photon excitation with a laser λ em = 750 nm was used.

FLIM data recorded from HEK293T cells expressing EYFP-G65T in the mitochondrial matrix. (A) Histograms showing the distribution of the probe's mean fluorescence lifetime in intact mitochondria (black solid line) and after the addition of 10 μM CCCP (red solid line) and 5 μM oligomycin A (blue solid line). (B) Calibration curve (part of the τm/pH dependence measured in the mitochondrial matrix and fitted with a 4th order polynomial regression curve) used to determine the absolute pH values in mitochondria. The mean lifetime and corresponding pH values are shown in the boxes. (C) Color-coded FLIM scans exemplifying the lifetime values recorded in the mitochondria of HEK293T cells before and after the addition of 10 μM CCCP and 5 μM oligomycin A (color legend is under the image gallery). Two-photon excitation with a laser λ em = 750 nm was used.

Effects of glucose starvation on mitochondrial pH. FLIM data were recorded from HEK293T cells expressing EYFP-G65T in the mitochondrial matrix. (A) Histograms showing the distribution of the probe's mean fluorescence lifetime in mitochondria under different conditions. Normal conditions (control): Transfected cells were plated overnight in complete high-glucose DMEM (25 mM glucose). Glucose starvation: The high-glucose medium was replaced with complete low-glucose DMEM (4 mM glucose) and incubated for the next 2 h. Recovery after starvation: The low-glucose medium was replaced with high-glucose medium, and the fluorescence lifetime was measured at several-minute intervals. ∗∗∗P < 0.001, ∗P = 0.012 for the one-way ANOVA test (n = 5). (B) A calibration plot (the τm/pH dependence measured in the mitochondrial matrix and fitted with a 4th order polynomial regression curve) used to determine the absolute pH values in mitochondria. The mean lifetimes and corresponding pH values are shown in the boxes. (C) Color-coded FLIM scans exemplifying the lifetime values recorded in the mitochondria of HEK293T cells under different conditions (color legend is provided below the image gallery). Since the medium was replaced during the measurements, different fields of view for the control and time points are shown. Two-photon excitation with a laser λ em = 750 nm was used.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent protein with environmentally-sensitive fluorescence lifetime for quantitative pH measurement

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Intracellular pH is a key factor in cell homeostasis, regulated within specific compartments, and changes in pH can result from or affect biochemical pathways. This study explores a yellow fluorescent protein EYFP-G65T as a core for a time-resolved pH-indicator. Among the tested designs—a circular permutant, a chimeric SypHer3s-like construct, and an unmodified protein—the unmodified EYFP-G65T performed best for live-cell imaging. Upon two-photon excitation, purified EYFP-G65T exhibited a 4.5-fold increase in mean fluorescence lifetime across pH 5.5–7 and a 7-fold change in its major component's lifetime from pH 6.5–8. Using this indicator, we measured pH values ranging from 6 to 8 in various organelles, and mapped pH shifts in mitochondria and the Golgi apparatus in response to stimuli.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The specific acidity of organelles and intracellular structures is among the factors essential for maintaining cellular homeostasis and controlling physiological processes within the cell [1,2]. In addition to the homeostatic maintenance of a specific pH value, the dynamics of pH are also of enormous physiological and biochemical importance. Thus, pH shifts accompany biological processes such as cell growth, proliferation, adhesion, cytoskeleton reorganization, cell volume regulation, secondary messenger activation, and apoptosis [3,4], with protons serving as both an archaic intra- and intercellular messenger [5,6] and a specific secondary messenger [7,8]. Measuring the value of intracellular pH and precisely recording its changes in situ can provide valuable insights into cellular metabolism and a deeper understanding of physiological and pathological processes [2,4,5,9].

Optical methods for sensing pH hold a valuable place among the various approaches to pH-metry [10]. Intracellular pH probes are represented by a palette of fluorescent molecules, including small organic dyes [6,11,12], fluorescence nanomaterials such as quantum dots [13], and genetically encoded indicators, which are primarily based on GFP-like fluorescent proteins (FPs) [14–18]. Although each family of pH-responsive fluorescent probes has its own advantages, genetically encoded indicators are particularly superior in terms of spatial specificity and minimal invasiveness for live-cell imaging [10,19].

Conventionally, the signal from fluorescent pH-indicators is acquired in what is known as the spectral domain; that is, its primary characteristic is the intensity at a specific wavelength(s). In the simplest case, signal readout is conducted at a single excitation/emission wavelength, and such probes are called intensiometric [20–28]. A more complex readout is employed for ratiometric indicators, where the signal is the ratio of fluorescence intensities measured at two λex/λem pairs [16–18,29–36]. Although ratiometric probes are less susceptible to artifacts arising from photobleaching, fluctuations in gene expression, and the peculiarities of imaging instruments, both types of indicators are widely used in biomedical research, including in vivo experiments [10,19,37]. Since the sum of extrinsic factors, such as the filter set, objective, and detector specifications, is involved in measuring fluorescence intensity, ‘spectral domain' indicators are used more to record relative changes in pH rather than determining its absolute value [20–23]. Concurrently, acquiring fluorescence signals in the time domain, as opposed to the spectral domain, offers broader opportunities for quantitative analysis in fluorescence biosensing [38]. Indeed, the kinetics of radiative decay that a fluorophore exhibits in a specific physicochemical environment is an intrinsic ensemble property, which is only weakly dependent on the instrumental aspects of measurement [39]. To date, numerous indicators that rely on time-resolved fluorescence readout have been described. These include those used for measuring membrane voltage [40,41], sodium [42], calcium [43], and copper [44] ion concentrations. There have also been efforts to use fluorescence lifetime for in cellulo pH measurements with fluorophores represented by organic dyes [45], quantum dots [46], FPs [14,47–50], and even an endogenous flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD), whose fluorescence lifetime has been shown to be pH-dependent [51]. Interestingly, lifetime-based genetically encoded pH indicators are primarily constructed from single FP molecules. In contrast, many advanced 'spectral domain' indicators [52,53] are chimeric proteins with more complex structures, such as circularly permuted FPs [54,55]. Specifically, SypHer3s [16], a brightness-enhanced member of the SypHer [56] family of ratiometric pH indicators, is based on the modified hydrogen peroxide probe HyPer (HyPer-C199S) [56,57] with intentionally blocked H2O2 sensitivity. HyPer, in turn, was engineered by the insertion of circularly permuted yellow fluorescent protein (cpYFP) into OxyR-RD, an H2O2-sensitive regulatory domain of the bacterial transcription factor OxyR [57]. Recent studies [19,58] suggest the utility of SypHer-family indicators in time-domain pH-metry. Considering that highly desirable properties, such as sensitivity across the entire range of intracellular pH (5–8) and a large (≫ 1 ns) pH-dependent change in fluorescence lifetime, are barely combined in the genetically encoded time-resolved pH indicators described to date [59], the issue of finding a fluorescent core and/or molecular architecture of the indicator that provides such a combination of properties remains relevant.

In our previous work, we described EYFP-G65T, a variant of the widely used yellow fluorescent protein (EYFP), which exhibits remarkable spectral sensitivity to environmental changes, particularly those of an electrostatic nature [60]. Specifically, this protein demonstrated a drastic change in the absorbance ratio between its protonated and anionic chromophore states in response to shifts in chloride ion concentration. Additionally, a notable change in the fluorescence lifetimes of the emissive species, when excited at both absorbance peaks, was observed [60]. Here, we examine the pH-sensitivity of EYFP-G65T and derived chimeric proteins, with a focus on time-domain measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Engineering of circular permutant of EYFP-G65T
ce:section.ce:para: DNA vectors containing the cpEYFP-G65T coding sequence were constructed by generating a tandem repeat (a technique similar to those described earlier [61]) of the EYFP-G65T coding sequences, connected via an oligonucleotide linker that encodes the amino acid sequence Gly-Gly-Ser-Gly-Gly-Thr-Gly. This repeat was synthesized using the overlap-extension PCR technique with the following oligonucleotides: forward 5′-ATCGGGATCCATGGTGAGCAAGGGCGAGGA-3'/reverse 5′-GGAACCCGTTCCTCCCGACCCCCCCTTGTACAGCTCATCCATCCC-3′ and forward 5′-TCGGGAGGAACGGGTTCCAGCAAGGGCGAGGAGCT-3'/reverse 5′-ATCGAAGCTTTTACTTGTACAGCTCGTCCATGCCG-3'. The PCR program for the amplification of the gene copies was: 95 °C for 5 min, followed by 18 cycles of (95 °C for 30 s, 57 °C for 30 s, 72 °C for 30 s), and a final extension at 72 °C for 7 min. The overlap PCR program was: 95 °C for 5 min, followed by 20 cycles of (95 °C for 30 s, 61 °C for 30 s, 72 °C for 30 s), and a final extension at 72 °C for 7 min. This intermediate construct was then used as a template for the PCR amplification of DNA sequences encoding cpEYFP-G65T. The forward (5′-end) primer was designed to match the sequence encoding the start of the C-terminal part of the first cpEYFP-G65T copy (starting from amino acid 149): 5′-ATCGGGATCCCACAATGTGTACATAATGGCAG-3′. The reverse (3′-terminal) primer was complementary to the DNA sequence encoding the end of the N-terminal part of the second cpEYFP-G65T copy (amino acids 3–144): 5′-ATCGAAGCTTGGAGTTGTAATTGTATTCCAACTTGTG-3′. The PCR program employed was: 95 °C for 5 min, followed by 20 cycles of (95 °C for 30 s, 52 °C for 30 s, 72 °C for 30 s), and a final extension at 72 °C for 7 min.
For bacterial expression, a PCR-amplified BamHI/Hin dIII fragment was cloned into the pQE30 vector (Qiagen).

The SypHer3s-G65T mutant was generated using the IVA PCR technique [62] with the following oligonucleotide primers containing the appropriate substitution: 5′-ACCCTCGTGACCACCCTCACCTACGGCCTGAAGTGCTTC-3′ and 5′-GAGGGTGGTCACGAGGGTGGGCCAGGGCACGGGCAG-3′. PCR reaction was conducted using Phusion® High-Fidelity DNA polymerase (NEB), according to the following protocol: 30 s at 95 °C, 18 cycles of (10 s at 95 °C, 30 s at 60 °C, 4 min at 72 °C), and a final 7 min extension at 72 °C. The addition of 1 μL FastDigest DpnI enzyme (Thermo Fisher Scientific) was followed by a 20 min incubation at 37 °C prior to transformation.

For mammalian cell expression (diffused cytoplasmic protein localization), PCR-amplified AgeI/NotI fragments (with forward 5′-ATCGACCGGTGCCACCATGGTGAGCAAGGGCGAGG-3′/reverse 5′-ATCGGCGGCCGCTTACTTGTACAGCTCGTCCATGCC-3′ and forward 5′-AGTCACCGGTTTCGCCACCATGTCCGGACCGCTGCAC-3′/reverse 5′-AGTCGCGGCCGCTTAAACCGCCTGTTTTAAAACTTTA-3′) encoding EYFP-G65T and SypHer3s-G65T, respectively, were cloned into the pEGFP-N1vector backbone (Clontech) instead of EGFP.

For mitochondrial expression, a PCR-amplified AgeI/NotI fragment encoding EYFP-G65T (with forward 5′-ATCGACCGGTCGCCACCATGGTGAGCAAGGGCGAGG-3′ and reverse 5′-ATCGGCGGCCGCTTACTTGTACAGCTCGTCCATGCC-3′) was cloned into the pTagRFP-mito vector backbone (Evrogen) instead of TagRFP. The mitochondrial targeting sequence (MTS) was derived from the subunit VIII of human cytochrome c oxidase [63–65].

For Golgi expression, a PCR-amplified BamHI/NotI fragment encoding EYFP-G65T (with forward 5′-ATGCGGATCCACCGGTCGCCACCATGGTGAGCAAGGGCGAGG-3′ and reverse 5′-ATCGGCGGCCGCTTACTTGTACAGCTCGTCCATGCC-3′) was cloned into the pTagRFP-golgi vector backbone (Evrogen) instead of TagRFP. The Golgi targeting sequence (GTS) was derived from a fragment of human β-1,4-galactosyltransferase [61,64].

The PCR programs used in all cases were: 95 °C for 5 min, followed by 20 cycles of (95 °C for 30 s, Tm (depending on the primer pair) for 30 s, 72 °C for 30 s), and a final extension at 72 °C for 7 min.

Protein coding sequences in the pQE30 vector backbone (Qiagen), featuring an N-terminal 6xHis tag, were expressed in the E.coli XL1 Blue strain (Invitrogen). The expression was carried out for 16 h at 37 °C followed by 24 h at 25 °C for EYFP-G65T, and for 24 h at 37 °C followed by 12 h at 25 °C for SypHer3s and SypHer3s-G65T. After centrifugation, the bacterial biomass was resuspended in PBS (Gibco, ThermoFisher Scientific, Waltham, Massachusetts, USA) pH 7.4 and treated with ultrasound by a Sonics Dismembrator (Fisher Scientific, Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania, USA). The proteins were isolated using TALON metal-affinity resin (Clontech Laboratories, Takara Bio USA, Inc., Palo Alto, CA, USA). The supernatant from bacterial lysis was loaded into a glass column (approximately 15 mL in total volume, with 1 mL of suspension). The column was pre-equilibrated with 10 column volumes of PBS. The protein-rich fraction of the cell lysate was incrementally added to facilitate the binding to the TALON resin. For subsequent fluorescence spectroscopy experiments, the spherical resin particles with immobilized proteins were washed four times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS pH 7.4, Gibco) at a 1:20 v/v ratio. The proteins were then solubilized using 100 mM imidazole pH 8.0 and desalted by ultrafiltration with Amicon® Ultra-0.5 10 K (Merck) columns (the final buffer was PBS pH 7.4).

The absorbance and fluorescence spectra of SypHer3s and SypHer3s-G65T were recorded using a Cary100 UV/VIS spectrophotometer and a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, California, USA), respectively. The samples with equal concentration of proteins were diluted in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4, Gibco). Their absorption spectra were measured in the range of 350–600 nm. Fluorescence excitation spectra were recorded at λem = 540 nm, and fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at λex = 485 nm. For the fluorescence recordings,the absorbance of the proteins was adjusted to be below 0.1.

To determine the operational pH range for SypHer3s, SypHer3s-G65T, and EYFP-G65T, a set of pH buffers in the pH range of 3.0–11.0 was used for steady-state fluorescence spectroscopy. Each buffer contained 130 mM potassium gluconate, 20 mM sodium gluconate, 0.5 mM MgCl2, 0.2 mM EGTA, and 30 mM of either sodium citrate (pH 3.0–4.5), MES (2-Morpholinoethanesulphonic acid) (pH 5.0–6.5), HEPES (pH 7.0–8.0), or Tris (pH 8.5–11.0). For the sample preparation, aliquots of proteins were dissolved in the pH-buffer at a 1:20 v/v ratio to a final concentration of 5 μg/mL. The signals of the probes SypHer3s/SypHer3s-G65T were measured as an F500/F420 ratio. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of each sample was recorded using an Agilent Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (at λem = 535 nm). For EYFP-G65T probes, fluorescence spectra were recorded upon excitation at 480 nm.

To measure the dependence of the fluorescence lifetime of purified SypHer3s-G65T and EYFP-G65T on pH, a set of pH buffers ranging from 5.5 to 10.0 was used to prepare samples for time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy. Each buffer contained 130 mM potassium gluconate, 20 mM sodium gluconate, 0.5 mM MgCl2, 0.2 mM EGTA, and 30 mM of either MES (2-Morpholinoethanesulphonic acid) (pH 5.5–6.5), HEPES (pH 7.0–8.0), or Tris (pH 8.5–10.0). For sample preparation, aliquots of proteins were dissolved in the pH buffer at a 1:20 v/v ratio to a final concentration of 5 μg/mL. The fluorescence decay of the probes was recorded, fitted by exponential models, and the fluorescence lifetime values were extracted based on the fitting procedure.

Additionally, to evaluate the influence of chloride anions on the pH-dependent fluorescence response of EYFP-G65T, the same set of buffers, supplemented with various concentrations of chloride anions (representing the physiologically relevant range of 2.5–100 mM), was used for lifetime measurements (under two-photon excitation only). To achieve the required concentration of chloride anions, an aliquot of 200 mM NaCl stock solution was added to each buffer at the necessary v/v ratio.
Time-resolved measurements were conducted using three different instrumental setups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Single-photon fluorescence excitation at 420 and 498 nm
ce:section.ce:para: Femtosecond laser pulses (80 MHz repetition rate, 100 fs, up to 25 nJ per pulse) from a Ti:Sapphire oscillator (Tsunami, Spectra-Physics) were selected at an 8 MHz repetition rate by an acousto-optic modulator (Pulse select, APE) and frequency-doubled in a BBO SHG crystal. Then femtosecond pulses with central wavelengths of 420 nm and 498 nm were coupled to an inverted optical microscope (IX71, Olympus) via a dichroic mirror (DMLP505, Thorlabs) and successfully focused by an objective lens (40× 0.75NA UPlanFLN, Olympus) onto a sample. The samples were prepared as droplets of purified fluorescent proteins dissolved in a buffered solution (pH 5.5–10) applied onto a standard 24 × 24 mm cover glass (Heinz Herenz, Germany). The average laser power was adjusted with a polarizing attenuator within the range of 0.1–10 μW. The fluorescence (>505 nm) passed back through the objective lens and laser-coupling dichroic mirror was directed to the input of an Acton SP300i monochromator with a hybrid single-photon detector on its output (HPM-100-6, Becker&Hickl), registering the fluorescence decay kinetics in the 510–520 nm band. Fluorescence decay data were analyzed using SPCImage software (Becker&Hickl, Germany) with the measured instrument response function (IRF).

Measurements were conducted using a time-resolved miniTau fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) within a 50-ns window divided into 2048 time channels. Fluorescence was excited by an EPL-450 picosecond laser (Edinburgh Instruments) with a central emission wavelength of 445.6 nm and a repetition rate of 20 MHz; photons were counted in the spectral range of 475–525 nm. Data processing and visualization, determination of χ2 (Pearson's test) were performed using the Fluoracle 2.5.1 software (Edinburgh Instruments).

The fluorescence decay data for EYFP-G65T were recorded using a single-photon counting (SPC) detector (HPM-100-40C, Becker & Hickl, Germany), within the spectral range of 500–650 nm limited by edge pass optical filters (Thorlabs). An oil immersion objective (PLAN APO 60×/1.4 NA, Nikon) was used for fluorescence excitation. Two-photon fluorescence was excited at 750 nm (repetition rate 80 MHz, pulse width 150 fs, optical power 20 mW) using a femtosecond optical parametric oscillator (TOPOL-1050-C, Avesta Project LTD.) pumped by a Yb femtosecond laser (TEMA-150, Avesta Project LTD.). Post-processing and visualization of the calculated data were performed using Origin Pro 2015 (OriginLab Corporation, USA).

HEK293T cells (EMBL collection) were seeded onto 35-mm glass-bottom dishes (MatTek or Fluorodish) and cultured in DMEM (PanEco company, Russia) with 10 % FBS (BioSera, Nuaille, France) at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 atmosphere (Series 8000 Water-Jacketed CO2 Incubator, Thermo Scientific, USA). After 24 h, cells were transfected with a mixture of 2 μg of DNA and 6 μl of FuGene HD transfection reagent (Promega Corporation, USA) in 100 μl OptiMEM (Gibco) solution per dish. After 12 h, the cell medium was replaced with fresh DMEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Permeabilization of cells with ionophores and setting of extrinsic pH values
ce:section.ce:para: To measure the dependence of the indicator's fluorescence lifetime on pH across a broad range of extrinsically set pH values (for use in a calibration plot to determine the absolute pH value), the cell membranes were permeabilized with a mixture of ionophores. Preparation for FLIM scanning of the ionophore-penetrated HEK293T cells expressing EYFP-G65T was performed as described in Ref. [35]. Each pH-buffer contained 5 μM nigericin, 5 μM monensin, 130 mM potassium gluconate, 20 mM sodium gluconate, 0.5 mM MgCl2, 0.2 mM EGTA, and 30 mM sodium citrate (pH 4.5), MES (2-Morpholinoethanesulphonic acid) (pH 5.0–6.5), HEPES (pH 7.0–8.0), or Tris (pH 8.5). For each pH point, cell dishes were washed twice and incubated for 4 min in the corresponding calibration buffer at 37 °C; thereafter, fluorescence lifetime measurements were taken.

Additionally, to evaluate the influence of chloride anions on the indicator's pH-dependent fluorescence response, the same set of calibration buffers, supplemented with various concentrations of chloride anions (representing the physiologically relevant range of 2.5–100 mM), was used for lifetime measurements. To achieve the required concentration of chloride anions, an aliquot of 200 mM NaCl stock solution was added to each buffer at the necessary v/v ratio.

To stimulate intracellular pH fluctuations, the intact, non-ionophore-permeabilized HEK293T cells expressing EYFP-G65T were treated with specific compounds known for their impact on cell physiology. To induce pH shifts in mitochondria, the cells were incubated with 10 μM CCCP (Carbonyl cyanide m-chlorophenylhydrazone, Sigma Cat#C2759) or 5 μM oligomycin A (Sigma Cat#75351). To induce pH shifts in the Golgi lumen, the cells were incubated with 10 μM CCCP or 25 mM NH4Cl. After the addition of a specific compound to the cell culture dish, it was incubated for 2 min at room temperature, followed by the recording of fluorescence lifetime values.

Transfected cells were plated overnight in complete high-glucose DMEM (25 mM glucose), then this medium was replaced with complete low-glucose DMEM (4 mM glucose) (PanEco company, Russia), and the cells were incubated in it for the next 2 h. After a 2-h incubation, the low-glucose medium was replaced with high-glucose medium. The fluorescence lifetime was measured prior to glucose starvation, immediately after incubation in low-glucose medium, and after starvation. In the latter case, imaging was performed at the 3rd, 8th, 15th, 30th minutes until the initial lifetime value was restored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM) of live HEK293T cells

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence lifetime imaging measurements on live cultured cells were conducted at room temperature in the air using an inverted Eclipse Ti–U microscope equipped with a DCS-120 confocal scanning FLIM system and an HPM-100-40C hybrid single-photon detector (Becker & Hickl GmbH). An oil immersion objective (PLAN APO 60×/1.4 NA, Nikon) was used for image acquisition. Fluorescence was excited by two-photon pulses at 750 nm generated by the femtosecond optical parametric oscillator TOPOL (Avesta Ltd.) pumped by a Yb femtosecond laser TEMA-150 (Avesta Ltd.), driven at an 80 MHz repetition rate (pulse duration 140 fs). Fluorescence was detected in the range of 500–650 nm, limited by edge pass optical filters (Thorlabs). The image recording time did not exceed 3 min in any case. The SPCImage software (Becker & Hickl) was used to extract the lifetime distributions from the collected images. Data post-processing was conducted using Origin Pro 2015 (OriginLab Corporation, USA).

The fluorescence lifetime values corresponding to specific pH values were plotted and fitted with a 4th order polynomial regression curve (‘Analysis/Fitting/Polynomial fit’ tool in Origin Pro 2015 (OriginLab Corporation, USA)). This was done to establish the continuous pH dependence of the fluorescence lifetime observed in each studied compartment (cytoplasm, mitochondrial matrix, Golgi lumen). These curves served as calibration plots for each compartment to determine absolute pH values. To achieve this, the lifetime values obtained from subsequent experiments (intact live cells, cells after chemical stimulation of intracellular pH shifts and glucose starvation) were mapped onto the curve, and the pH values were calculated using the fitting equation.

For the analysis of the significance of fluorescence lifetime changes during the glucose starvation experiment a one-way ANOVA test (α < 0.05) (the GraphPad Prism 8.0.1 package) was used. For the analysis, the average lifetime was calculated from 3 to 5 technical replicates.

Genetic constructs. To compare pH-dependent photobehavior, we prepared DNA-constructs encoding an unmodified EYFP-G65T, its circularly permuted version (cpEYFP-G65T), and a modified indicator SypHer3s, whose original fluorescence core is based on cpEYFP [16] (Fig. 1 ). The permutation procedure was carried out at amino acid position 144, involving the deletion of a 4-amino-acid fragment (corresponding to positions 145–148 in the native protein) and the insertion of a GGSGGTG linker between the permuted chains. This method is similar to that previously used to generate the cpEYFP fluorescence core of well-characterized ratiometric indicators [16,53,66–68] (Fig. 1). For SypHer3s, a G65T (G260T according to SypHer3s numbering) point mutation was introduced into its coding sequence by site-directed mutagenesis (Fig. 1).

Optical properties and pH-dependent photobehavior of the purified proteins. We first expressed the aforementioned proteins and unmodified SypHer3s in a bacterial system, isolated and purified them for further characterization. cpEYFP-G65T was found to be almost non-fluorescent (data not shown) and was thus excluded from further examination. SypHer3s-G65T exhibited steady-state absorption and fluorescence properties similar to the parental protein, albeit with a noticeably enhanced amplitude of the short-wavelength excitation peak (Supporting Data Fig. S1). Under steady-state fluorescence spectrometry, SypHer3s-G65T exhibited pronounced pH sensitivity, particularly in the pH 7–11 range, where its fluorescence intensity at a single spectral channel (λex = 500 nm/λem = 525 nm) changed ∼ 8-fold (Supporting Data Fig. S2). In ratiometric mode, where the signal is represented by the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two excitation wavelengths (F500/F420), the signal of SypHer3s-G65T displayed a more than 15-fold increase upon a pH shift from 6.5 to 10.5 (Fig. 2 and Supporting Data Fig. S2). As expected, the alkalization of the buffer led to an increase in fluorescence intensity excited at 500 nm and a decrease at 420 nm. Notably, the modified SypHer3s retained a similar operational pH range to its parent protein but exhibited approximately a 2-fold relative decrease in the dynamic range of its ratiometric signal (Fig. 2).

As demonstrated previously, an aqueous solution of EYFP-G65T exhibits two primary absorption bands peaking at ∼ 400 nm and ∼ 510 nm [60], with only the latter enabling efficient fluorescence excitation. Thuswise, the amplitude of the 510 nm excitation peak, along with the corresponding 525 nm emission peak, was anticipated to serve as an intensiometric readout in EYFP-G65T-based pH-metry. Indeed, a roughly 25-fold change in fluorescence intensity at 525 nm was observed within the pH range of 5–11 (Supporting Data Fig. S3).

pH-sensitivity of fluorescence lifetimes in aqueous solution. Considering that both SypHer3s-G65T and EYFP-G65T have two absorbance maxima corresponding to the neutral and anionic forms of the GFP-type chromophore, we can anticipate that different excitation wavelengths will reveal mixtures of emissive species of varying composition, with differing relative contributions and/or lifetimes (similar to what was observed in our previous study on pH measurement using Fluorescence Lifetime Imaging Microscopy (FLIM) [59]). The sensitivity of the fluorescence lifetimes of these emissive species (and consequently of the mean fluorescence lifetime of a protein under study) on pH can vary, which makes the excitation wavelength a critical factor in determining the ultimate efficiency of pH-metry. Examining the properties of the indicator at different excitation wavelengths gains additional importance in light of a recent study [69], which has demonstrated the decisive impact of excitation wavelength on the apparent pKa, and thus on the operational pH range of the indicator. Therefore, our initial task was to identify the optimal excitation mode that offers the highest measurement sensitivity within the physiological pH range. To achieve this, we utilized two wavelengths of a tunable Ti-sapphire femtosecond laser (λem = 420 nm and λem = 498 nm), providing excitation that approximately corresponds to the absorption maxima for the neutral and anionic chromophore states, respectively. An alternative for the single-photon excitation mode was a picosecond laser (λem = 450 nm), offering excitation between the two main absorption peaks. Additionally, we employed two-photon excitation provided by a femtosecond laser emitting at 750 nm for this study. We incorporated the two-photon excitation mode into the experiment due to its principal advantages, such as reduced phototoxicity and enhanced imaging depth, which are particularly beneficial for biological microscopy involving thick tissue samples.

We measured the fluorescence decay kinetics for the protein samples at all specified excitation wavelengths. These samples included purified SypHer3s-G65T and EYFP-G65T, which were dissolved in a series of buffers ranging from pH 5.5 to 10, with increments of 0.5 pH unit. Given that the pH sensitivity of unmodified SypHer3s has been previously determined through time-resolved fluorescence microscopy [30], we did not include this protein in the current examination.

For SypHer3s-G65T, the fluorescence decay data obtained at all excitation wavelengths were satisfactorily fitted by a two-exponential model (Supporting Data Table S1, Supporting Data Figs. S4–S7). Based on these results, we did not observe substantial pH-dependent shifts in the fluorescence lifetimes of SypHer3s-G65T upon excitation at 498 nm and 450 nm. In contrast, the long-lived component (τ2) of the fluorescence emission upon excitation at 420 nm increased from ∼ 2700 ps–3100 ps within the pH range of 7.0–10.0, with the greatest lifetime changes observed in the 8.5–10.0 range (Supporting Data Table S1, Fig. S4). A similar behavior was observed in two-photon excitation mode at 750 nm, where the long-lived component (τ2) increased from approximately 1700 ps–2000 ps within the pH 9.0–10.0 range (Supporting Data Table S1, Fig. S7).

As with SypHer3-G65T, the fluorescence decay data obtained for EYFP-G65T with all excitation wavelengths used were satisfactorily fitted by a two-exponential model (Supporting Data Table S2, Supporting Data Figs. S8–S11). The exception was the 420 nm excitation mode, where a two-exponential fitting can only be applied for the 7.5–10 pH range. For adequate fitting of data in the pH range of 5.5–7.5, a three-component model can be used, describing the decay with two components similar to those found in the 7.5–10 range, and an additional minor extremely short-lived component (Supporting Data Table S3). As expected, the sensitivity of the fluorescence lifetime of EYFP-G65T to pH was found to be unequal in different excitation modes. Thus, with 420 nm excitation, there was approximately a 5-fold increase in the lifetime of the short-lived emissive species (τ1, from ∼ 400–2000 ps) in the 9.0–10 pH range (Fig. 3 A, Supporting Data Fig. S8). This photo-behavior variant is barely applicable for measuring pH in living cells due to the physiological irrelevance of the indicated pH range. With 450 nm excitation, the lifetime of the short-lived component increases almost 3-fold (τ1, from ∼ 1.25–3.5 ns) in the 5.5–8 pH range (Fig. 3A, Supporting Data Fig. S9). Excitation at 498 nm turned out to be the least effective for revealing pH-dependent changes in the fluorescence lifetime of EYFP-G65T. Specifically, in the pH range of 5.5–7.5, the short-lived component of the fluorescent decay (τ1) changed less than 1.5 times, from 1.6 to 2.1 ns (Fig. 3A, Supporting Data Fig. S10).

Finally, the 2P-750 nm excitation provided the best expression of the EYFP-G65T's fluorescence decay sensitivity to pH. Thus, under this excitation mode, the short-lived component of the biexponential fluorescence decay exhibited almost a 14-fold lifetime increase in the 6.0–8.0 pH range (τ1, from 250 to 3500 ps), which implies a much greater sensing contrast compared to other excitation wavelengths used (see Fig. 3A, Supporting Data Table S2, Fig. S11). In addition, the mean fluorescence lifetime (τm) of the EYFP-G65T at this excitation wavelength also displayed a well-defined pH-dependence and can probably be adequately used as a signal readout, while the changes of the mean lifetime upon other excitation modes are much less pronounced (see Fig. 3B). Within the excitation wavelength range we have examined, the pH-sensitivity of the mean fluorescence lifetime increases with decreasing wavelength (see Fig. 3B, Supporting Data Table S2). This may indicate an important contribution of excited-state processes of the chromophore's neutral form (for example, ESPT variants or chromophore conformational motions) to the manifestation of pH-sensitivity of EYFP-G65T fluorescence. In the case of two-photon excitation, this effect is likely enhanced due to the peculiarities of the two-photon absorption [70] of the studied protein.

Overall, EYFP-G65T demonstrated remarkable pH-sensing properties in an aqueous environment. On one hand, by combining different excitation modes and methods of time-resolved signal analysis, it can potentially be applied in a wide pH range (5.5–8 and 9–10.5), which can be beneficial for quantitative fluorescent pH-metry in cell-free systems. On the other hand, the total change in average lifetime by 2.6 ns in the pH range of 5.5–7 under two-photon excitation is, to our knowledge, the largest described in the literature for fluorophores of this type.
Taking the opportunity to couple the same 750 nm laser that was used for the spectroscopy of purified proteins, to a FLIM microscope, we moved on to measurements on mammalian cells.

Static pH measurements in cellulo. To evaluate the pH-sensitivity of the fluorophores in cellulo in the time domain, we conducted FLIM imaging in HEK293T cells. First, we aimed to determine the reference fluorescence lifetime values that the indicator possesses at different pH values when distributed in the cytoplasm. To this end, live cells expressing the indicator with non-specific intracellular (diffused cytoplasmic) localization were incubated with an ionophore mixture prior to each imaging series. The role of the ionophores was to assess the permeability of the plasma membrane to the extracellular solution, allowing for the subsequent replacement of the culture medium with a pH buffer (pH ranging from 4.5 to 8.5, with the same composition as that used for the protein specimens) to extrinsically set the intracellular pH value.

While measurements on the purified protein indicated that the fluorescence lifetime of SypHer3s-G65T changes slightly within the physiologically relevant pH range, we decided to reassess its pH sensitivity in a cellular model. FLIM data revealed that in HEK293T cells, the mean fluorescence lifetime of this indicator variant is almost pH-insensitive, fluctuating around 1800–1900 ps (Supporting Data Fig. S12).

The FLIM data demonstrate that EYFP-G65T fluorescence is pH-sensitive within the entire physiologically relevant range (Fig. 4 ). Importantly, the mean fluorescence lifetime's pH-dependence curve in HEK293T cells (Fig. 4B) noticeably deviates from that measured on the purified protein (Fig. 3B). It aligns more closely with the pH-dependence of EYFP-G65T's short-lived emissive species (τ1, Fig. 3A), which indeed was shown to dominate in the EYFP-G65T's fluorescence decay measured in HEK293T cells. Interestingly, there is a pronounced shift (1–1.5 pH units) in the pH-sensitivity between the intracellular and purified indicator, a phenomenon previously reported in several studies [49,59]. We attribute this to the manifestation of the cellular content's residual buffering capacity. The pH-dependence of EYFP-G65T's mean fluorescence lifetime exhibits a bimodal pattern, marked by a steep rise from 600 to 1700 ps between pH 5.0 and 5.5—likely the chromophore's apparent pKa region—and a gradual increase from 1700 to 2700 ps between pH 5.5 and 7.5. However, distinguishing the indicator's signal between pH 7.5 and 8.5 is challenging (see lifetime distribution histograms, Fig. 4A), establishing EYFP-G65T's tentative operational pH range as 5–7.5, with a total lifetime increase of more than 2 ns, which is one of the greatest ever documented for FP-based indicators. Of note, the chloride anion, previously shown to shorten the fluorescence lifetime of purified EYFP-G65T at a concentration of ∼140 mM upon single-photon excitation at 400 nm [60], showed a negligible effect on the mean fluorescence lifetime of both purified and intracellular indicator in our two-photon measurements, where it was examined in the physiological concentration range (2.5–100 mM) across all pH values tested (Supporting Data Fig. S13).

Profiles of pH sensitivity and signal calibration across different compartments. The key feature of eukaryotic cells is compartmentalization, which allows cells to perform various tasks with remarkable speed and specificity. Each compartment maintains a unique resting pH essential for its efficient functioning. Consequently, measuring the absolute pH value in different compartments of a living cell in real-time, including multiple compartments simultaneously, is highly relevant.

Considering that EYFP-G65T showed different pH-sensitivity profiles in protein solution and during cellular expression, we decided to first check how its fluorescence lifetime behaves in specific subcellular structures under conditions of extrinsically set pH. To this end, we genetically targeted EYFP-G65T to the mitochondrial matrix and the trans-region of the Golgi apparatus by fusing its coding sequence with the mitochondrial targeting sequence (MTS) derived from subunit VIII of human cytochrome с oxidase, and the Golgi targeting sequence (GTS), which is a fragment of human β-1,4-galactosyltransferase, respectively. We then FLIM-visualized the indicator-expressing cells after pre-treating them with ionophores and pH buffers, similarly to how we previously imaged cells with the non-specific cytoplasmic localization of EYFP-G65T. We selected the subcellular structures in such a way that their native pH values represented different sub-ranges of physiologically relevant acidity: mildly acidic in the case of the trans-Golgi and mildly alkaline in the case of the mitochondrial matrix (while the cytoplasm is expected to have a near-neutral pH on average) [1,63].

Our analysis revealed distinct pH-dependence curves for EYFP-G65T, localized in different cellular compartments (Fig. 5 ). In particular, in the trans-Golgi, the greatest lifetime contrast shifted to the more alkaline pH range (5.5–6.5 compared to 5.0–5.5 in the cytoplasm). These distinctions pertain to both the pH range exhibiting the greatest changes in mean fluorescence lifetime and the specific lifetime values at given pH levels. While the former distinction is probably explained by the difference in the inherent buffering capacity of the organelle content (affecting the apparent pKa of the chromophore), the latter may also be a consequence of differences in the refractive index [71], characteristic for each specific organelle. Overall, these findings highlight the limitations of this indicator, particularly the impracticality of applying a universal calibration plot for quantitative analysis. Apparently, it is essential to calibrate the fluorescent response of EYFP-G65T within each specific model system to accurately determine absolute pH values. On the other hand, the shift in the maximum pH sensitivity range could be beneficial by broadening the overall indicator's operational range.

Using the lifetime-pH dependence curves as calibration plots, we determined the absolute pH values in live intact cells expressing the indicator in the cytoplasm, Golgi lumen, and mitochondrial matrix (Fig. 5). The values obtained (∼6.3 in the Golgi, ∼ 7.2 in the cytoplasm, and ∼ 7.6 in the mitochondria) were consistent with literature data [2], thus confirming the potential applicability of EYFP-G65T for intracellular pH-metry. It should be noted, however, that the correct pH value for each organelle could be calculated only if the organelle-specific calibration plot was used. In this context, the suitability of EYFP-G65T for the simultaneous measurement of pH in various subcellular structures remained unclear.

To evaluate the indicator's potential for multi-target pH measurements, we co-expressed its two targeted variants—cytoplasmic and mitochondrial—and visualized them in HEK293T cells with extrinsically set pH levels (ranging from 6.5 to 8.0), aiming to calibrate their signals simultaneously.

The indicator's subcellular fractions displayed well-distinguishable fluorescence lifetimes across all pH points examined (Fig. 6 A–C), ensuring that the resulting calibration plots did not overlap (Fig. 6B). Importantly, the calibration plots for both cytoplasmic and mitochondrial variants in this experiment closely matched those previously measured for individual localizations (see Figs. 6 and 7 B, and Supporting Data Fig. S14). Therefore, the absolute pH values in the cytoplasm and mitochondria of intact, live HEK293T cells, as determined by this dual-target calibration, were as accurate to one decimal place as those previously discussed (Supporting Data Fig. S14).

Dynamic pH measurements in cellulo. Monitoring intracellular pH dynamics is pivotal for cellular physiology and biochemistry research, as shifts in acidity often indicative of metabolic activities. However, this task is technically challenging due to the typically minor pH fluctuations, which may require detecting changes as subtle as 0.1 pH units. Therefore, an advanced fluorescent pH indicator should be capable of determining the absolute pH value and discerning even the smallest alterations. In this study, we employed two approaches to induce pH changes in living cells, subsequently tracked with EYFP-G65T. The first implied chemical stimulation of biochemical pathways, while the second entailed creating stress conditions, both anticipated to lead to acidity shifts.

It is known that the resting pH of the Golgi lumen is primarily determined by three distinct ion transport systems. These include the vacuolar (V)-ATPase-mediated proton pump, counter ion (chloride ion, Cl−) transport, and proton ‘leak' across the Golgi membranes back into the cytoplasm [61]. We treated the cells expressing EYFP-G65T with a Golgi-targeting signal with two reagents anticipated to affect these ion transport systems. Specifically, the exogenous conductive protonophore, carbonyl cyanide m-chlorophenyl hydrazone (CCCP), is known to accelerate the dissipation of the pH gradient, which is expected to result in the alkalization of the Golgi lumen [64]. After treating the cells with 10 μM CCCP, we indeed detected a drastic increase in the pH of the Golgi apparatus (from pH 6.3 to 7.1, as shown in Fig. 7). A similar, though less pronounced, effect (alkalization from pH 6.3 to 6.5, Fig. 7) was induced by the addition of 25 mM ammonium chloride (NH4Cl). This effect, the magnitude of which (0.2 pH units) closely matches that previously described in the literature for HeLa cells [72], can, in turn, be explained by the ‘neutralizing’ action of a weak base (ammonium cation), which, upon entering the cell, leads to a disruption of the pH gradient over the secretory pathway [61]. The alteration of the indicator's signal caused by chloride anions, whose concentration in the cell significantly increases after the addition of NH4Cl, cannot be ruled out either. However, our control experiments (Supporting Data Fig. S13) show that their impact should be extremely weak.

In mitochondria, we successfully induced both acidification and alkalization of the matrix content and recorded these pH changes with high precision using the mitochondrially-targeted EYFP-G65T. Due to the slightly alkaline resting pH of the mitochondrial lumen, the effect of the CCCP ionophore on the pH value was opposite to that observed in the Golgi lumen, where the resting pH is mildly acidic. Thus, the addition of 10 μM CCCP led to a strong acidification of the mitochondrial matrix, with EYFP-G65T recording a shift from pH 7.6 to 6.4 (Fig. 8 ). This change aligns with the uncoupling effect of CCCP, which moves protons by increasing the permeability of lipid membranes [65]. Conversely, the mitochondrial toxin oligomycin A induced a slight alkalization of the matrix (from pH 7.6 to 7.8, Fig. 8), an effect attributable to its inhibition of ATP synthase and proton translocation, resulting in matrix alkalization. The relatively small amplitude of the observed pH shift is likely linked to the activity of intrinsic mitochondrial uncoupling, which facilitates the influx of protons into the mitochondrial matrix bypassing ATP synthase [73].

To conclude, we effectively recorded chemically induced pH fluctuations within two subcellular structures. Notably, EYFP-G65T has demonstrated efficiency in sensing both slight and substantial pH variations, and encompassing both acidification and alkalization processes.

Glucose starvation. Finally, we examined the impact of glucose starvation on mitochondrial pH. Depletion in glucose was a way to induce stress in living cells, which closely modeled the conditions they may encounter in vivo.

We first measured the mitochondrial pH in HEK293T cells grown normally in DMEM culture medium, then switched to a low-glucose variant of DMEM, which contained approximately 6 times less glucose, and repeated the measurement after 2 h of incubation in the glucose-depleted environment. The cells subjected to glucose starvation exhibited a noticeable increase in mitochondrial pH values compared to the normal cells (from pH 7.44 to 7.9, Fig. 9 ). We attribute this behavior to the phenomenon known as aerobic glycolysis. A key characteristic of this effect is the increased lactate production in the cytosol. Activation of NHE (Na+/H+ exchangers) and MCT (Monocarboxylate Transporters) by mitogenic kinases leads to an elevation in cytosolic pH [31]. Alkalization of the cytosol results in a reduction of protons in the intermembrane space of mitochondria, diminishing the H+ gradient across the inner membrane and causing matrix alkalization. This occurs because the outer mitochondrial membrane contains voltage-dependent anion channels (VDAC), which are permeable to protons [32].

The addition of glucose after starvation led to a gradual decrease in the pH of the mitochondrial matrix back to the initial value, occurring over a timescale of minutes (Fig. 9A–C). This is explained by the proton-dependent transport of pyruvate: the addition of glucose stimulates the accelerated formation of pyruvate. The transfer of pyruvate into the mitochondrial matrix requires a transporter, which operates using the symport of protons [33]. Furthermore, an increase in pyruvate leads to an increase in the products of the Krebs cycle. The export of these products ensures the operation of the electron transport chain and the synthesis of ATP, due to the translocation of protons into the matrix.

In this study, we have demonstrated that the unmodified fluorescent protein EYFP-G65T can function as a pH indicator, capable of determining the absolute pH value in living cells with high precision. Concurrently, other molecular designs based on it, such as a circular permutant and a SypHer3s-G65T construct, were ineffective for various reasons. The former does not fluoresce, likely due to hindrances in folding and/or chromophore maturation, while the fluorescence lifetime of the latter is practically insensitive to pH within the physiologically relevant range. At the purified protein level, unmodified EYFP-G65T exhibited high pH sensitivity in its fluorescence. In the spectral domain, it showed approximately a 25-fold change in fluorescence intensity in response to a pH shift from 5 to 11 upon single-photon excitation at 510 nm. In the time domain, with two-photon excitation at 750 nm, the short-lived component of its biexponential fluorescence decay demonstrated almost a 14-fold lifetime increase in the 6.0–8.0 pH range (from 250 to 3500 ps), and its mean fluorescence lifetime underwent a 4-fold change in the 5.5–7.5 range (750–3350 ps). In living cells, both the overall working pH range and the range of optimum pH sensitivity were highly dependent on the intracellular localization of the indicator (refer to Figs. 5 and 6). Generally, it can be stated that the indicator is operable within the entire physiological pH range of 5–8, but the greatest pH-dependent lifetime changes—and thus the highest measurement accuracy (over 1 ns per pH unit)—were achieved in the acidic region: pH 5–6 in the cytoplasm and pH 5.5–6.5 in the Golgi apparatus. Although alkaline pHs (7.5–8) fall outside the optimal range of EYFP-G65T, we successfully detected and recorded very small changes in the pH of the mitochondrial matrix during glucose starvation. The wide dynamic and pH measurement ranges make this indicator one of the best in its class (see Supporting Data Table S4 for comparison).
